parts.prompt:
On the Lewis diagram in part A, circle the atom that accepts the proton when the glycolate ion reacts with water.

When the glycolate ion reacts with water, it forms glycolic acid, $HC_2H_3O_3$, according to the following equation. The $K_b$ expression for the reaction is provided.

$C_2H_3O_3^-$(aq) + $H_2O$(l) ‚áĆ $HC_2H_3O_3$(aq) + $OH^-$(aq)     $K_b$ = $\frac{[HC_2H_3O_3][OH^-]}{[C_2H_3O_3^-]}$
parts.scoring_notes: The glycolate ion has a carboxylate group (‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ). Either of the two equivalent carboxylate O atoms may be circled; both are equivalent by resonance.

scoring_points.description: For circling either carboxylate oxygen atom (proton acceptor): 1 point

required_elements: One of the two carboxylate O atoms in the ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ group is circled.
alternatives.correct_example: The carboxylate group (‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ) acts as the proton acceptor (Br√łnsted‚ÄďLowry base). Because of resonance, both C‚ÄďO bonds in ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ are equivalent; either O atom may be circled.

wrong_examples: Circling the ‚ÄďOH oxygen of glycolate ‚ÄĒ that O is already bonded to H and would be a donor, not acceptor.
Circling a carbon atom ‚ÄĒ carbon cannot easily accept a proton in this context.
common_traps: Proton acceptor = Br√łnsted‚ÄďLowry base; the carboxylate O is the basic site.

parts.prompt:
At 25¬įC, a 2.5 M solution of glycolate is found to have [$OH^-$] = 1.3 √ó 10$^{-5}$ M.

i. Calculate the value of $K_b$ for the glycolate ion.

ii. Using your answer to part B(i), calculate the value of $K_a$ for glycolic acid at 25¬įC.

Glycolic acid can be produced from the hydrolysis of methyl glycolate, $C_3H_6O_3$. A proposed mechanism for the reaction is given.

Step 1: $C_3H_6O_3$ + $H_3O^+$ ‚áĆ $C_2H_5O_3^+$ + $CH_3OH$
Step 2: $C_2H_5O_3^+$ + $H_2O$ ‚áĆ $HC_2H_3O_3$ + $H_3O^+$
Overall: $C_3H_6O_3$ + $H_2O$ ‚áĆ $HC_2H_3O_3$ + $CH_3OH$
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): Kb = [HC‚āāH‚āÉO‚āÉ][OH‚ĀĽ]/[C‚āāH‚āÉO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ]. At equilibrium [HC‚āāH‚āÉO‚āÉ] ‚Čą [OH‚ĀĽ] = 1.3√ó10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĶ; [C‚āāH‚āÉO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ] ‚Čą 2.5 (small x assumption valid). Part (b)(ii): Ka √ó Kb = Kw = 1.0√ó10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī ‚Üí Ka = Kw/Kb.

scoring_points.description: For correct Kb calculation and correct Ka using Kw relationship: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using [OH‚ĀĽ] = 1.3√ó10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĶ in the denominator ‚ÄĒ it belongs in the numerator.
Adding Ka + Kb = Kw instead of multiplying.
common_traps: For a conjugate acid-base pair at 25¬įC: $K_a \times K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$.

parts.prompt: A student claims that $H_3O^+$ is a catalyst for the reaction. Do you agree or disagree? Justify your answer based on the mechanism given.
parts.scoring_notes: Agree. H‚āÉO‚Āļ is consumed in step 1 (reactant) and regenerated in step 2 (product). It does not appear in the overall equation ‚ÄĒ consistent with a catalyst.

scoring_points.description: For 'agree' with valid justification citing consumption and regeneration of H‚āÉO‚Āļ in the mechanism: 1 point

required_elements: H‚āÉO‚Āļ is consumed in step 1 and regenerated in step 2.
It does not appear in the overall equation ‚ÄĒ consistent with catalyst behavior.
alternatives.correct_example: Agree. A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a reaction without being consumed overall. In step 1, H‚āÉO‚Āļ is consumed, but in step 2 it is regenerated. H‚āÉO‚Āļ does not appear in the overall equation ($C_3H_6O_3 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HC_2H_3O_3 + CH_3OH$), confirming it acts as a catalyst.

wrong_examples: Disagree ‚ÄĒ H‚āÉO‚Āļ clearly reappears in step 2 and is absent from the net equation.
Agree, because H‚āÉO‚Āļ appears in step 1 ‚ÄĒ must also note regeneration in step 2.
common_traps: A catalyst must be both consumed in an early step AND regenerated in a later step; demonstrating only one is incomplete.
